column5: The pathophysiology of allergic contact dermatitis is characterized by an initial sensitization phase followed by an elicitation phase. The sensitization phase involves the epicutaneous exposure to an allergen@ which is processed by epidermal Langerhans cells and presented to T cells in regional lymph nodes. This leads to the development of allergen-specific T cells. The elicitation phase occurs upon subsequent exposure to the same allergen@ triggering an inflammatory response mediated by the previously sensitized T cells. This response results in the characteristic symptoms of allergic contact dermatitis@ such as redness@ itching@ and blistering.

column5: Allergic diseases are immune system disorders characterized by exaggerated responses to otherwise harmless substances (allergens). These reactions can manifest in various forms@ including skin reactions (eczema@ hives)@ respiratory issues (asthma@ allergic rhinitis)@ gastrointestinal problems (food allergies)@ and systemic reactions (anaphylaxis). The underlying mechanism involves sensitization to an allergen@ leading to the production of IgE antibodies that bind to mast cells and basophils. Upon subsequent exposure@ the allergen cross-links the IgE@ triggering the release of inflammatory mediators such as histamine@ leukotrienes@ and cytokines@ causing the characteristic symptoms of allergic reactions.

column35: Allergic reactions occur when the immune system mistakenly identifies a harmless substance as a threat. This triggers the production of IgE antibodies that bind to mast cells. column5: The major histocompatibility complex (MHC) class I antigen B*27 is a cell-surface glycoprotein consisting of a heterodimer@ composed of a heavy chain and a light chain (beta-2 microglobulin). The heavy chain is anchored in the membrane. MHC class I molecules play a central role in the immune system by presenting peptides derived from cytosolic proteins. They are expressed in nearly all cells. The heavy chain contains three domains: alpha1@ alpha2@ and alpha3. The alpha1 and alpha2 domains form the peptide-binding groove. The alpha3 domain interacts with the CD8 coreceptor. Genetic variations in MHC class I genes are associated with susceptibility to different diseases. HLA-B*27 is strongly associated with ankylosing spondylitis.
